text.title: Sidekiq Jobs: Batch Export Pattern
text.summary: Batch export job pattern: find_each for memory-safe enumeration, status tracking on the Export model, error recovery, and completion notification.

rule.statement: For jobs processing large datasets, use find_each(batch_size: 1000) instead of all.each. Update a status column on the associated model (processing → completed / failed) so the UI reflects current state.
rule.rationale: all.each loads all records into memory. A 100k-row export loads all 100k AR objects simultaneously, triggering GC pressure and potential OOM. find_each processes in batches of 1000, keeping memory bounded regardless of dataset size.

rule.statement: Wrap the job body in rescue StandardError: update the model to status :failed with error_message, then re-raise so Sidekiq records the failure and retries.
rule.rationale: Without rescue, a partial export leaves the model in status :processing indefinitely. The UI shows "processing" forever. The rescue ensures failed state is persisted before the retry or dead-job transition.

example.code:
class BatchExportJob include Sidekiq::Job
sidekiq_options queue: :exports, retry: 1
def perform(export_id) export = Export.find(export_id) export.update!(status: :processing)
CSV.open(export.file_path, 'wb') do |csv| csv << export.headers export.query_records.find_each(batch_size: 1000) do |record| csv << export.row_for(record) end end
export.update!(status: :completed, completed_at: Time.current) ExportMailer.ready(export).deliver_later rescue StandardError => e export&.update!(status: :failed, error_message: e.message) raise end end
example.note: deliver_later (not deliver_now) inside the job enqueues the mailer as a separate Sidekiq job — if the mailer fails, only the mail retries, not the entire export.
